You are Hermes Agent, an intelligent AI assistant created by Nous Research. You are helpful, knowledgeable, and direct. You assist users with a wide range of tasks including answering questions, writing and editing code, analyzing information, creative work, and executing actions via your tools. You communicate clearly, admit uncertainty when appropriate, and prioritize being genuinely useful over being verbose unless otherwise directed below. Be targeted and efficient in your exploration and investigations.

Conversation started: Sunday, April 12, 2026 01:08 AM
Model: anthropic/claude-opus-4.6
Provider: openrouter

You are on a text messaging communication platform, Telegram. Please do not use markdown as it does not render. You can send media files natively: to deliver a file to the user, include MEDIA:/absolute/path/to/file in your response. Images (.png, .jpg, .webp) appear as photos, audio (.ogg) sends as voice bubbles, and videos (.mp4) play inline. You can also include image URLs in markdown format ![alt](url) and they will be sent as native photos.

function.name: patch
function.description:
Targeted find-and-replace edits in files. Use this instead of sed/awk in terminal. Uses fuzzy matching (9 strategies) so minor whitespace/indentation differences won't break it. Returns a unified diff. Auto-runs syntax checks after editing.

Replace mode (default): find a unique string and replace it.
Patch mode: apply V4A multi-file patches for bulk changes.

mode.description: Edit mode: 'replace' for targeted find-and-replace, 'patch' for V4A multi-file patches

path.description: File path to edit (required for 'replace' mode)

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'replace' mode). Must be unique in the file unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'replace' mode). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: Replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false)

patch.description:
V4A format patch content (required for 'patch' mode). Format:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: path/to/file
@@ context hint @@
 context line
-removed line
+added line
*** End Patch

function.name: process
function.description: Manage background processes started with terminal(background=true). Actions: 'list' (show all), 'poll' (check status + new output), 'log' (full output with pagination), 'wait' (block until done or timeout), 'kill' (terminate), 'write' (send raw stdin data without newline), 'submit' (send data + Enter, for answering prompts).

action.description: Action to perform on background processes

session_id.description: Process session ID (from terminal background output). Required for all actions except 'list'.

data.description: Text to send to process stdin (for 'write' and 'submit' actions)

timeout.description: Max seconds to block for 'wait' action. Returns partial output on timeout.

offset.description: Line offset for 'log' action (default: last 200 lines)

limit.description: Max lines to return for 'log' action

function.name: read_file
function.description: Read a text file with line numbers and pagination. Use this instead of cat/head/tail in terminal. Output format: 'LINE_NUM|CONTENT'. Suggests similar filenames if not found. Use offset and limit for large files. Reads exceeding ~100K characters are rejected; use offset and limit to read specific sections of large files. NOTE: Cannot read images or binary files — use vision_analyze for images.

path.description: Path to the file to read (absolute, relative, or ~/path)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed, default: 1)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read (default: 500, max: 2000)

function.name: search_files
function.description:
Search file contents or find files by name. Use this instead of grep/rg/find/ls in terminal. Ripgrep-backed, faster than shell equivalents.

Content search (target='content'): Regex search inside files. Output modes: full matches with line numbers, file paths only, or match counts.

File search (target='files'): Find files by glob pattern (e.g., '*.py', '*config*'). Also use this instead of ls — results sorted by modification time.

pattern.description: Regex pattern for content search, or glob pattern (e.g., '*.py') for file search

target.description: 'content' searches inside file contents, 'files' searches for files by name

path.description: Directory or file to search in (default: current working directory)

file_glob.description: Filter files by pattern in grep mode (e.g., '*.py' to only search Python files)

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return (default: 50)

offset.description: Skip first N results for pagination (default: 0)

output_mode.description: Output format for grep mode: 'content' shows matching lines with line numbers, 'files_only' lists file paths, 'count' shows match counts per file

context.description: Number of context lines before and after each match (grep mode only)

function.name: terminal
function.description:
Execute shell commands on a Linux environment. Filesystem usually persists between calls.

Do NOT use cat/head/tail to read files — use read_file instead.
Do NOT use grep/rg/find to search — use search_files instead.
Do NOT use ls to list directories — use search_files(target='files') instead.
Do NOT use sed/awk to edit files — use patch instead.
Do NOT use echo/cat heredoc to create files — use write_file instead.
Reserve terminal for: builds, installs, git, processes, scripts, network, package managers, and anything that needs a shell.

Foreground (default): Commands return INSTANTLY when done, even if the timeout is high. Set timeout=300 for long builds/scripts — you'll still get the result in seconds if it's fast. Prefer foreground for short commands.
Background: Set background=true to get a session_id. Two patterns:
  (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers).
  (2) Long-running tasks with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working on other things and the system auto-notifies you when the task finishes. Great for test suites, builds, deployments, or anything that takes more than a minute.
Use process(action="poll") for progress checks, process(action="wait") to block until done.
Working directory: Use 'workdir' for per-command cwd.
PTY mode: Set pty=true for interactive CLI tools (Codex, Claude Code, Python REPL).

Do NOT use vim/nano/interactive tools without pty=true — they hang without a pseudo-terminal. Pipe git output to cat if it might page.
Important: cloud sandboxes may be cleaned up, idled out, or recreated between turns. Persistent filesystem means files can resume later; it does NOT guarantee a continuously running machine or surviving background processes. Use terminal sandboxes for task work, not durable hosting.

command.description: The command to execute on the VM

background.description: Run the command in the background. Two patterns: (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers). (2) Long-running tasks paired with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working and get notified when the task finishes. For short commands, prefer foreground with a generous timeout instead.

timeout.description: Max seconds to wait (default: 180). Returns INSTANTLY when command finishes — set high for long tasks, you won't wait unnecessarily.

workdir.description: Working directory for this command (absolute path). Defaults to the session working directory.

check_interval.description: Seconds between automatic status checks for background processes (gateway/messaging only, minimum 30). When set, I'll proactively report progress.

pty.description: Run in pseudo-terminal (PTY) mode for interactive CLI tools like Codex, Claude Code, or Python REPL. Only works with local and SSH backends. Default: false.

notify_on_complete.description: When true (and background=true), you'll be automatically notified when the process finishes — no polling needed. Use this for tasks that take a while (tests, builds, deployments) so you can keep working on other things in the meantime.

function.name: write_file
function.description: Write content to a file, completely replacing existing content. Use this instead of echo/cat heredoc in terminal. Creates parent directories automatically. OVERWRITES the entire file — use 'patch' for targeted edits.

path.description: Path to the file to write (will be created if it doesn't exist, overwritten if it does)

content.description: Complete content to write to the file

[Subdirectory context discovered: /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/AGENTS.md]
# Law Firm Wiki — Compiler Schema

> This file is the specification for the LLM that compiles activity logs
> into structured knowledge articles. Read it in full before any compile
> or query operation.

## What You Are

You are a knowledge compiler for a personal injury law firm. Your job is
to read raw case activity logs and extract reusable institutional knowledge
— the process, pitfalls, patterns, and best practices that make a PI
practice work.

You are NOT creating a case database. You are building a **wiki** — a
structured collection of concept articles about HOW PI cases work, derived
from evidence in real cases.

## The Three Operations

### 1. COMPILE (ingest raw → update wiki)

Read activity logs and case files. For each meaningful pattern you identify:

1. Check `index.md` — does a concept article already exist?
2. If yes → UPDATE it: add new evidence, adjust confidence, update Related
3. If no → CREATE it: full article per the Article Schema below
4. If two concepts interact → create/update a Connection article
5. Update `index.md` with any new or modified articles
6. Append a summary to `log.md`

**What to extract:**
- Process patterns (what happens and in what order)
- Decision points (why certain choices were made)
- Problems and resolutions (what went wrong, how it was fixed)
- Timeline patterns (how long things actually take)
- Communication patterns (what works with adjusters, clients, providers)
- Edge cases (unusual situations and how they were handled)
- Anti-patterns (things that caused problems)

**What to IGNORE:**
- Individual client PII (names, SSN, DOB, addresses, phone numbers)
- Specific dollar amounts tied to identifiable cases
- Attorney-client privileged communications
- Raw email addresses and phone numbers
- Replace specifics with anonymized references: "Case A", "Case B", or
  descriptive patterns: "in cases involving [treatment gap > 30 days]"

**Confidence scoring:**
- high: Pattern observed in 5+ cases with consistent outcomes
- medium: Pattern observed in 2-4 cases
- low: Single case observation, or conflicting evidence

### 2. QUERY (ask questions → synthesize answers)

1. Read `index.md` to identify 3-10 relevant concept articles
2. Read those articles in full
3. Synthesize an answer with [[wikilink]] citations
4. If the answer reveals a gap → note it in the answer
5. Optionally file the Q&A back into `wiki/qa/`

### 3. LINT (health check)

Run these checks and write results to `wiki/reports/`:
- **Broken wikilinks**: Links that point to non-existent articles
- **Orphan articles**: Concepts with zero inbound links
- **Phase coverage**: Which PHASE_DAG phases have sparse knowledge
- **Stale articles**: Source data changed since last compile
- **Contradictions**: Articles that contain conflicting guidance

## Article Schema

### Concept Article (wiki/concepts/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Human-Readable Title
slug: kebab-case-slug
phase: [phase_0_onboarding]    # which PHASE_DAG phases this applies to
category: one-of-below
tags: [tag1, tag2, tag3]
confidence: high               # high/medium/low
evidence_count: 5              # number of cases with supporting evidence
created: YYYY-MM-DD
updated: YYYY-MM-DD
related:                       # other concept slugs
  - related-concept-one
  - related-concept-two
---

# Title

> One-paragraph summary (2-3 sentences max)

## Pattern

Detailed explanation of the pattern, process, or concept.

## Evidence

Anonymized observations from source cases:
- "In N cases where [condition], [outcome]"
- "When [action was taken], [result]"

## Agent Guidance

Actionable instructions for paralegal agents:
1. Step one
2. Step two
3. Step three

## Pitfalls

- Common mistake one
- Common mistake two

## Related

- [[related-concept-one]] — how it connects
- [[related-concept-two]] — how it connects
```

### Categories

- `process`: Standard workflows and procedures
- `adjuster-tactics`: Insurance company behavior patterns
- `edge-cases`: Unusual situations requiring special handling
- `phase-knowledge`: Phase-specific best practices
- `communication`: Client/adjuster/provider communication patterns
- `financial`: Billing, liens, settlements, funding
- `medical`: Treatment patterns, records, chronologies
- `legal`: Litigation, discovery, depositions, trial
- `tools`: Software, systems, and process tools

### Connection Article (wiki/connections/*.md)

Same schema but `category: connection` and the body explains how
two or more concepts interact. Example:

```
treatment-gaps-affect-demand-value.md
→ Links: treatment-gap-defense + demand-timing-strategy
→ Insight: Cases with documented gap explanations recovered 80% of
  expected value vs 50% for unexplained gaps
```

### Q&A Article (wiki/qa/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Question text
slug: question-slug
date: YYYY-MM-DD
sources:                       # concept articles consulted
  - concept-slug-one
  - concept-slug-two
---

# Question

[Full question text]

## Answer

[Synthesized answer with [[wikilink]] citations]

## Gaps Identified

[Any knowledge gaps the question revealed]
```

## Index Schema (wiki/index.md)

The master catalog. This is the LLM's entry point for all queries.

```markdown
# Law Firm Wiki — Index

Last compiled: YYYY-MM-DD | Articles: N | Connections: N

## By Phase

### Phase 0: Onboarding
| Article | Category | Confidence | Evidence |
|---------|----------|------------|----------|
| [[concepts/onboarding-pitfalls]] | phase-knowledge | high | 15 |

### Phase 1: File Setup
...

## By Category

### Adjuster Tactics
| Article | Phase | Confidence |
|---------|-------|------------|
| [[concepts/treatment-gap-defense]] | 2,3 | high |
...

## Recent Updates

| Date | Article | Action |
|------|---------|--------|
| 2026-04-12 | treatment-gap-defense | created |
```

## Activity Log Format Reference

Activity logs live at `cases/<slug>/Activity Log/*.md` and have this structure:

```yaml
---
schema_version: 2
case_id: uuid
date: "YYYY-MM-DD"
time: "HH:MM:SS"         # optional
category: legal|correspondence|imported|financial|medical|task
subcategory: email|filevine_negotiation|outbound_call|...
---

# Category — Date

**Case:** [[cases/slug/slug|Client Name]]
**Source:** description

[Free-form content: emails, notes, call summaries, task descriptions]
```

### Subcategory Distribution (21K+ logs)
- email: 3,078
- filevine_negotiation: 1,620
- outbound_call: 890
- missed_call: 780
- filevine_treatment: 717
- filevine_demandinprogress: 645
- demand: 534
- filevine_filesetup: 369
- phase_change: 254
- filevine_uim: 179
- inbound_call: 137
- esignature: 78
- call_summary: 10

## PHASE_DAG Reference

The case lifecycle has 8 phases:
- Phase 0: Onboarding (contract, HIPAA, intake)
- Phase 1: File Setup (insurance claims, provider setup)
- Phase 2: Treatment (monitoring, records collection)
- Phase 3: Demand (drafting, review, sending)
- Phase 4: Negotiation (offers, counters, strategy)
- Phase 5: Settlement (agreement, disbursement)
- Phase 6: Lien Resolution (PIP waterfall, lien negotiation)
- Phase 7: Litigation (complaint, discovery, trial)
- Phase 8: Closed

## Rules

1. NEVER include identifiable client information in wiki articles
2. Anonymize all case references (use "Case A", "Case B", or descriptive patterns)
3. Every article must have at least one [[wikilink]] to a related concept
4. Update index.md whenever you create or modify an article
5. Append to log.md whenever you compile
6. Confidence scores must reflect actual evidence count
7. When in doubt about a pattern's validity, use confidence: low
8. Prefer specific actionable guidance over vague advice
9. Group knowledge by PHASE_DAG phase when possible
10. Flag contradictions explicitly — don't silently resolve them

[Subdirectory context discovered: /opt/data/FirmVault/CLAUDE.md]
# FirmVault — Agent Orientation

> **Read this in full at the start of every session.** It names the thesis, the non-negotiable rules, and where to find the authoritative docs. Short deliberately — use the imports below for detail.

## What this repo is

FirmVault is the **development environment for an AI paralegal backend** intended to eventually be sold to other personal-injury law firms. This specific repo is Whaley Law Firm's own instance; the architecture we prove here becomes the product.

Two parallel product tracks exist:

1. **This repo** — the GitHub-native paralegal backend: markdown vault, `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, `SKILL.md` library, runtime (materializer + worker), intake pipeline.
2. **Separate Huly fork** (not in this repo) — the lawyer-facing UI. This repo's "UI" is for operators, not lawyers.

## The thesis

**"The firm as a code repo."** Law firm work is repeatable tasks + templates + rules, which is exactly what code is. Put the firm's work into primitives agents already excel at (issues, branches, PRs, diffs) and you get a much better AI experience than any traditional PMS bolt-on.

## Core architectural principles

1. **The vault is a shadow, not the source of truth.** Real files (signed PDFs, audio, emails, faxes) live on the firm's own storage (their server, Dropbox, Box, Drive). The markdown in `cases/<slug>/` is a PHI-masked projection of those files.
2. **Intake is a pipeline, not an agent**: document arrives → real file placed on user's storage → converted to markdown → PHI stripped → opens a PR against this repo. The PR is the first human-in-the-loop gate.
3. **Git is the deliberate interface.** Every agent action is a commit or PR. Free audit trail, free approval gates, trivial rollback. Agents *never* touch real files — only their masked shadows.
4. **Markdown, not a graph DB.** We tried FalkorDB and abandoned it. LLM agents are empirically more reliable on plain text + grep than on Cypher queries. This is a deliberate choice, not laziness.
5. **PHI never lives in the vault.** Placeholders like `{{client_ssn}}` and `{{dob}}` reference secrets held in the user's own infrastructure. At render time, deterministic code (not the model) substitutes real values. The model never sees raw PII.
6. **The vault is the only state store for this layer.** No external database. No per-case JSON files. No FalkorDB. No `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`. See `@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md` for the authoritative mapping.

## Do NOT do these things

These are actively deprecated. If you see them in old code or docs, treat them as bugs.

- **Do not reference FalkorDB, Cypher, `graph_client`, `graph_manager`, or `run_cypher_query`.** All removed. Everything reads the vault.
- **Do not reference per-case JSON files** (`overview.json`, `contacts.json`, `insurance.json`, `liens.json`, `expenses.json`, `medical_providers.json`, `case_state.json`). Deleted.
- **Do not reference `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`** or any absolute-path scaffolding. Everything is repo-relative.
- **Do not edit content between `<!-- roscoe-medical-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-medical-end -->` or `<!-- roscoe-insurance-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-insurance-end -->` markers.** Those blocks are owned by the import scripts and will be overwritten on next sync.
- **Do not invent new vault paths.** If the thing you want to write doesn't have a home in `DATA_CONTRACT.md`, update the contract first (same commit), then write. Drift between contract and reality is the bug.
- **Do not create top-level dashboards, reports, or state files outside the vault layout**. Operator views read the vault via GitHub API or git; they don't create parallel state.

## Where the authoritative docs live

Import these at launch — they are the contract, not me paraphrasing:

@DESIGN.md

@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md

@MEMORY.md

Also non-imported but worth knowing:

- `skills.tools.workflows/workflows/PHASE_DAG.yaml` — machine-readable phase definitions, landmarks, and predicate vocabulary. The materializer reads this.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/README.md` — how the materializer and worker agents drive the task queue.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/task_schema.md` — the contract for task issues (labels, frontmatter, lifecycle).
- `Templates/INDEX.md` — the firm template library (73 slugified templates grouped by category).
- `skills.tools.workflows/Skills/*/SKILL.md` — 42 modernized skills in Anthropic Agent Skills format. Exemplars: `lien-management`, `deposition-prep`, `deposition-strategy`, `pip-application`.

## Slug rules (critical for wikilinks)

From `DATA_CONTRACT.md` §4:

- Lowercase
- Apostrophes and curly quotes **stripped** (no replacement char): `K'daya` → `kdaya`
- `&` → `and`
- All other non-alphanumeric runs collapsed to a single hyphen
- Leading/trailing hyphens stripped

Mismatched slugs break wikilinks across the vault. Slug before you write.

## Runtime model (what's being built)

The system is being turned from passive documentation into an active work-driving loop:

```
Vault (cases/*) ← materializer (cron) → GitHub Issues ← worker (event) → Vault
```

- **Materializer** walks open cases, reads each case's `landmarks:` frontmatter + `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, opens a GitHub Issue for every unsatisfied landmark whose task template preconditions are met. Already prototyped as a prompt in `runtime/materializer_prompt.md`.
- **Worker** is triggered by the `status:ready` label on an issue, claims it, loads the named skill, runs it, commits vault changes that reference the task_id, and flips the issue status. Prototyped in `runtime/worker_prompt.md`.
- **Source of truth** for whether a landmark is satisfied is `case.frontmatter.landmarks.<id>` — the materializer checks this first and only falls back to evaluating a predicate if the key is missing.

## Working branch

All in-progress work lives on `claude/import-cases-from-drive-xiQgL`. Do not push directly to `main` without an explicit request.

## When in doubt

- Read `DATA_CONTRACT.md` before writing to the vault.
- Read `PHASE_DAG.yaml` before touching workflow/landmark logic.
- Read `MEMORY.md` to find out what's in flight and what's pending.
- Check the last 3–5 commits on the working branch to see the most recent decisions.

MAINTAINER NOTES (stripped before injection; free context)

This file is intentionally short. If it starts approaching 200 lines, split
topic-specific rules into `.claude/rules/*.md` and reference them here.

Do not duplicate DATA_CONTRACT.md content — @-import it. The contract is
authoritative; this file is orientation.

MEMORY.md is a regular repo file we @-import here. It is NOT the same as
Claude Code's auto-memory (~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/MEMORY.md) —
that's machine-local and managed by Claude Code itself. Our repo-level
MEMORY.md is the operational state tracker, committed to git.
-->

# Intake and Onboarding Workflow

> The intake process converts a potential client into an active case. It involves collecting personal information, executing the retainer agreement, setting up insurance claims, identifying medical providers, and establishing communication channels. The workflow must be completed rapidly to preserve evidence and begin the claims process.

# Case Phase Transitions

> PI cases follow a defined lifecycle: Lead -> Potential New Client -> File Setup -> Treatment -> Demand in Progress -> Negotiation -> Settlement. Each transition has specific prerequisites and is triggered by specific events. Understanding these transitions is critical for case management automation.

# Medical Records and Bills Collection

> Collecting complete medical records and bills from all treatment providers is a prerequisite for demand preparation. This process involves sending HIPAA authorizations, formal record/bill requests via fax and email, and persistent follow-up when providers are slow to respond. Gaps in records collection directly delay case resolution.

# Negotiation After Demand

> After a demand is sent, the case enters negotiation with the insurance adjuster. This phase involves waiting for and evaluating the initial offer, communicating with the client about the offer, potential counter-offers, and either settlement or escalation to litigation. The negotiation phase can be complicated by client dissatisfaction, offer rejection, and lien resolution.

# Underinsured Motorist (UIM) Claim Management

> When the at-fault driver's BI policy limits are insufficient to cover the plaintiff's damages, the plaintiff may pursue an Underinsured Motorist (UIM) claim against their own auto insurance carrier. UIM claims run as a parallel track to the BI claim and require distinct procedural steps including consent-to-settle notifications, separate complaint filing, and independent adjuster management — often with the same carrier that provides the client's PIP coverage.

# Companion Case Management

> When multiple family members are injured in the same accident, each client requires a separate case file but the cases must be coordinated. Companion cases share the same defendant, insurance carriers, and litigation timeline, but each plaintiff has independent medical treatment, damages, and settlement negotiations. The primary client (typically the parent/driver) often serves as the coordinator for all cases.

# Client Communication Through Family Members

> In many PI cases, the primary point of contact is a family member (spouse, parent) rather than the injured client directly. This pattern creates both efficiencies (an engaged advocate) and risks (communication gaps, authorization requirements). Managing these relationships requires clear protocols around authorization, documentation, and expectation-setting.

# Statute of Limitations Monitoring

> The statute of limitations (SOL) is the absolute deadline for filing a lawsuit. In Kentucky PI cases, this is typically 2 years from the date of accident. Approaching the SOL without resolution requires escalation to the attorney for a decision: settle at current offer, file suit, or withdraw. SOL monitoring is automated in Filevine with countdown alerts.

# Workers' Compensation Case Patterns

> Workers' compensation cases differ fundamentally from personal injury cases in their procedural framework, involved parties, and resolution mechanisms. WC cases are governed by state Department of Workers' Compensation (DWC) rules rather than civil court procedures, involve employer's WC insurance carriers rather than at-fault BI carriers, and resolve through administrative proceedings (Benefit Review Conferences) rather than jury trials.

# Workers' Compensation Claim Patterns

> Workers' compensation (WC) cases follow a fundamentally different process than auto accident PI cases. WC claims involve an employer's insurance carrier that must approve treatment, authorize specific providers, and pay Temporary Total Disability (TTD) benefits. Unlike BI claims where the firm collects records and sends a demand, WC cases require ongoing adjuster interaction to authorize each step of treatment.

# Client Frustration Escalation Pattern

> PI clients exhibit a predictable pattern of escalating frustration when they feel the firm is not communicating with them. This pattern typically begins with polite check-ins, escalates to demands for immediate response, and can culminate in threats to change attorneys. Preventing this escalation through proactive communication is more effective than responding to it after it begins.

# Unreachable Client Patterns

> Some PI clients are persistently difficult to reach — their voicemail is full, they don't return calls, and emails go unanswered. This pattern is distinct from client frustration (where the client is actively trying to reach the firm). Unreachable clients create a bottleneck because the firm needs their input to proceed with intake, treatment confirmation, and case management. Persistent multi-channel contact attempts are required, sometimes over weeks.

# Adjuster Persistent Unreturned Calls Pattern

> Insurance adjusters sometimes repeatedly call the firm, leaving multiple voicemails over weeks or months without getting a return call. This pattern can occur on both sides: adjusters trying to settle may be unable to reach the attorney, or the firm may deliberately deprioritize a carrier's calls while focused on other aspects of the case. Both directions carry risk.

# Health Insurance Lien Management

> When a PI client has health insurance that pays accident-related medical bills, the health insurer asserts a subrogation lien — a right to be reimbursed from any settlement. Managing these liens involves verifying their accuracy, removing unrelated charges, and ultimately negotiating a reduction. Liens directly reduce the client's net recovery.

# Demand Preparation Workflow

> The demand phase begins when the client's treatment is complete and all medical records/bills have been collected. The transition to "Demand in Progress" is a formal phase change that triggers a series of tasks: final records requests, records review for completeness and relatedness, lien identification, and ultimately the drafting and sending of the demand package to the insurance adjuster.

# Case Manager Handoff Patterns

> PI cases frequently outlast the tenure of the case manager who initially worked them. When a case manager leaves or cases are reassigned, the incoming staff member must pick up an unfamiliar file, establish rapport with the client, and continue without losing momentum. These handoffs create a risk of dropped tasks, communication gaps, and client frustration.

# Employer and Commercial Vehicle Liability

> When a PI case involves a commercial vehicle (taxi, transit bus, delivery vehicle) or an employer's vehicle, the liability analysis shifts from individual driver negligence to employer/respondeat superior liability. These cases name the employing entity (LLC, government agency) as a defendant and involve distinct defense postures including third-party claims administrators, government risk management, bifurcation motions, and specialized defense counsel.

# Multi-Carrier Multi-Defendant Case Management

> When an accident involves multiple at-fault parties (e.g., chain-reaction collisions), the plaintiff's firm must manage separate BI claims against each defendant's carrier simultaneously. Each carrier may have different adjusters, different liability postures, different policy limits, and different negotiation timelines. Coordination across these parallel tracks is critical to maximize recovery.

   630|### Key New Patterns Identified
   631|- **VA lien amounts can be massive**: $86K+ VA CCN-Optum lien dwarfs all private liens observed; VA lien resolution can take years during active litigation
   632|- **Three-carrier cases (BI+PIP+UIM) with VA overlay create extreme complexity**: Managing 4 parallel financial tracks (BI litigation + PIP benefits + UIM claim + VA lien) simultaneously
   633|- **Nuisance offers trigger strategic litigation pause**: When insurers make $500 offers on $5K demands, the firm pauses negotiation to build leverage through defendant depositions rather than countering
   634|- **Government transit defendants have distinct procedures**: Government attorneys (not adjusters) handle correspondence; risk management companies serve as intermediaries
   635|- **Employer/taxi liability is a distinct case type**: Commercial vehicle employer liability involves bifurcation motions, LLC defendants, and specialized defense counsel (LMS)
   636|- **Dual Medicare lien scenarios exist**: Medicare Advantage (Aetna Medicare) and traditional Medicare BCRC can both assert liens on the same case, requiring independent tracking
   637|- **Bifurcation of issues in PI litigation**: Defense tactic separating liability and damages into distinct trial phases — new litigation pattern not previously documented
   638|- **Closed cases still generate client frustration**: Clients receiving bills after settlement phase trigger a distinct frustration pattern driven by tangible financial pain rather than case progress
   639|- **Companion case depositions coordinate**: Both companions deposed same day at defense counsel office, with defendant deposed separately later
   640|- **Back-to-back redialing indicates peak frustration**: Clients calling back within 1-2 minutes of an unanswered call (observed in 3 cases) signals Stage 3+ frustration
   641|

function.name: read_file
function.arguments: {"path": "/opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/companion-case-management.md"}

messages.content:
{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Companion Case Management
     3|slug: companion-case-management
     4|phase: [phase_0_onboarding, phase_1_file_setup, phase_7_litigation]
     5|category: process
     6|tags: [companion, family, multiple-plaintiffs, coordination, deposition]
     7|confidence: medium
     8|evidence_count: 8
     9|created: 2026-04-12
    10|updated: 2026-04-12
    11|related:
    12|  - intake-and-onboarding-workflow
    13|  - client-communication-through-family
    14|  - litigation-process-patterns
    15|---
    16|
    17|# Companion Case Management
    18|
    19|> When multiple family members are injured in the same accident, each client requires a separate case file but the cases must be coordinated. Companion cases share the same defendant, insurance carriers, and litigation timeline, but each plaintiff has independent medical treatment, damages, and settlement negotiations. The primary client (typically the parent/driver) often serves as the coordinator for all cases.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|1. **Intake**: One family member signs up first; firm discovers additional family members during the welcome call or initial contact
    24|2. **File Creation**: Each companion gets a separate case file with separate intake documents
    25|3. **Coordination**: Parent/primary client is expected to facilitate the companion's document signing, provide their information (SSN, medical providers, insurance)
    26|4. **Records Collection**: Each companion's medical providers must be identified and records requested independently — this often falls on the primary client to facilitate
    27|5. **Litigation**: Companions may be joined in one lawsuit (e.g., "Plaintiff A et al. vs. Defendant") or filed separately. Depositions are scheduled sequentially for companions
    28|6. **Settlement**: Each companion's settlement is evaluated independently, but mediation may address all cases simultaneously
    29|
    30|## Evidence
    31|
    32|- In Case G (MVA), the client's daughter was identified as a companion case during the welcome call. Her Filevine file had not been created yet, requiring coordination. The primary client was asked repeatedly over 6+ months to obtain Children's Hospital records for the companion but failed to do so. Both cases were joined in one lawsuit (Boone 24-CI-00452). During mediation via Zoom, both cases received separate LBF (last best & final) offers. Mediation failed for both.
    33|- In Case I (MVA), a companion witness (Dana Jackson) was deposed alongside the client. Depositions were scheduled sequentially via Zoom — the client was told "we are going to do Dana's deposition first so just chill and I will shoot you a text when we are about done." Coordinating two depositions on the same day required active text communication to manage timing.
    34|- In 2 cases from this batch, companion cases were identified at intake: one MVA had a driver and passenger (mother/son) treated at the same ER, creating parallel files with shared BI carrier but separate demand tracks. The paralegal sent demands referencing both clients' records to the same BI adjuster. In another, a companion case was noted but the intake was sent to the companion "via vinesign" through a family member contact. Companion cases with shared chiropractic providers can have records/billing returned together, requiring manual separation.
    35|- In an MVA case (multi-plaintiff, Floyd County IN), three plaintiffs from the same accident were joined in one lawsuit. The complaint was filed as a multi-plaintiff action. Deposition scheduling required coordination across all plaintiffs through court reporting services. Mediation was being scheduled for all cases simultaneously. The case crossed state lines (KY firm filing in IN), adding jurisdictional complexity for companion case coordination. Two of the settled BI claims were resolved separately per plaintiff, confirming that settlement amounts are independent even in joint litigation.
    36|- In Case M (MVA, multi-plaintiff), three additional passengers in the same vehicle were each represented by different law firms (4 total plaintiffs). The at-fault driver's insurance tendered policy limits (25/50) to be divided among all four plaintiffs. The coordination challenge shifted from records collection to inter-counsel distribution: the firm had to email all three other firms to negotiate allocation. This process stalled for months (Jan-Apr 2024+) as counsel for one co-plaintiff was slow to provide medical bills needed for allocation. The delay frustrated the client, who called repeatedly asking for updates and was told "we are still waiting on a reply on the one person we're waiting for medical bills." When distribution could not be agreed upon, the case moved to litigation with all plaintiffs joined in one lawsuit. Depositions for multiple plaintiffs in the joint suit were coordinated through defense counsel.
    37|- In a bus accident case (DOI 7/2024), two passengers (companions) were injured in the same incident. Both were filed as separate cases but shared the same defendant (commercial transportation company + unknown driver) and the same insurance carrier. Settlement demands were sent for both companions simultaneously. Defense counsel corresponded about both clients in single email threads ("Abby Sitgraves & Nayram Adadevoh"). The companion filing was confirmed as "et al" in the complaint (Jefferson 25-CI-000133). Each companion had independent medical providers and separate health insurance liens. The out-of-state companion's records collection required different providers than the primary client.
    38|- In an MVA case (DOI 10/2023), the companion relationship was between spouses. The complaint was filed as a companion case in the same jurisdiction. Both clients shared PIP coverage but had separate BI claims. The firm tracked SOL independently for each companion.
    39|
    40|## Agent Guidance
    41|
    42|1. During intake, ask whether other family members were in the vehicle and injured
    43|2. Create separate case files for each companion immediately — do not delay
    44|3. Send intake documents to companions as early as possible
    45|4. Track records collection for each companion independently
    46|5. When the primary client is the gateway to companion information, set explicit deadlines and follow up
    47|6. For litigation, coordinate with the attorney on whether to file jointly or separately
    48|7. Schedule depositions for companions on the same day when possible to minimize coordination burden
    49|8. During mediation, prepare separate settlement evaluations for each companion
    50|
    51|## Pitfalls
    52|
    53|- Discovering companion cases days/weeks after the primary intake (records requests are delayed)
    54|- In Case R (auto accident, DOI 4/2023), the client was identified as a companion case at intake — a note explicitly stated "Companion: [family member]" with a link to the related Filevine project. Both clients were joined in the same lawsuit. Depositions were scheduled separately for both parties at the same defense counsel's office. The companion's case had a distinct demand amount. Both depositions included the defendant, confirming that companion case litigation coordinates plaintiff, companion, and defendant depositions. The case also demonstrates that companion cases may require separate demand evaluations (the companion's demand was for $5K on minor injuries while the related case could have a different amount).
    55|- Relying on the primary client to obtain companion's information without explicit deadlines
    56|- Not creating a separate case file (tracking companion in notes on primary case)
    57|- Not requesting companion's medical records independently (assuming they're covered by the primary request)
    58|- Scheduling conflicts when companions need to attend the same deposition/mediation session
    59|
    60|## Related
    61|
    62|- [[intake-and-onboarding-workflow]] — companion cases are discovered during intake
    63|- [[client-communication-through-family]] — primary client acts as intermediary for companions
    64|- [[litigation-process-patterns]] — companion cases may be joined in one lawsuit
    65|", "total_lines": 64, "file_size": 7848, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

The case confirmed that companion case mediation scheduling requires all plaintiffs' availability.

The firm sent records/billing for both clients to the BI adjuster in the same communication, and followed up on offers for both simultaneously. The BI claim settled first, triggering a COOTS letter, while the UIM track continued separately. This demonstrates the companion case pattern where one claim type settles while others remain open across the same companion set.
- In an MVA case (treatment phase), the case involved 3 separate BI carriers (two at-fault drivers plus a third carrier), with liability accepted by one carrier while negotiations continued with others. The multi-carrier structure meant that each companion's recovery depended on different liability determinations from different carriers, adding complexity beyond typical companion cases that share a single defendant."}

The companion filing was confirmed as \"et al\" in the complaint (Jefferson 25-CI-000133). Each companion had independent medical providers and separate health insurance liens. The out-of-state companion's records collection required different providers than the primary client.
 - In an MVA case (DOI 10/2023), the companion relationship was between spouses. The complaint was filed as a companion case in the same jurisdiction. Both clients shared PIP coverage but had separate BI claims. The firm tracked SOL independently for each companion.
+- In an MVA case (negotiation phase), a non-English-speaking client had a companion case that was not yet uploaded. The case manager noted they would "wait for the companions case to be uploaded and will make it as the point of contact," indicating that the companion case setup was blocking the primary's file setup. The companion case was referenced by a co-counsel attorney negotiating jointly, with three attorneys (from three firms) coordinating a multi-party negotiation with the same BI carrier. This shows that companion cases can involve inter-firm coordination, not just intra-firm coordination.
+- In an MVA case (litigation phase), three passengers from the same vehicle (client plus two companions) were represented by the same firm in a multi-plaintiff action filed in Floyd County. The lawsuit was styled as "et al. v. [Defendant]" and depositions were coordinated across all plaintiffs. Multiple BI carriers and a PIP carrier were involved, with each companion having independent claims against potentially different carriers. The case confirmed that companion case mediation scheduling requires all plaintiffs' availability.
+- In an MVA case (closed/settlement), two spouses were injured and filed companion cases against the same BI carrier, with shared PIP and UIM coverage through the same insurer. The firm sent records/billing for both clients to the BI adjuster in the same communication, and followed up on offers for both simultaneously. The BI claim settled first, triggering a COOTS letter, while the UIM track continued separately. This demonstrates the companion case pattern where one claim type settles while others remain open across the same companion set.
+- In an MVA case (treatment phase), the case involved 3 separate BI carriers (two at-fault drivers plus a third carrier), with liability accepted by one carrier while negotiations continued with others. The multi-carrier structure meant that each companion's recovery depended on different liability determinations from different carriers, adding complexity beyond typical companion cases that share a single defendant.
 
 ## Agent Guidance

# Employer Workers' Compensation Claim Obstruction

> When employers fail to initiate workers' compensation claims or actively resist them, injured employees face a compounding set of problems: employer-directed medical care at occupational clinics (e.g., Concentra) that prioritize return-to-work over treatment, forced use of PTO/vacation time instead of TTD benefits, and inability to get work restrictions accommodated. This pattern often drives the employee to seek legal representation.

## Pattern

1. **Workplace injury occurs** — employer transports employee to employer-directed occupational clinic (not employee's choice of provider)
2. **Occupational clinic minimizes**: Clinic examines only the primary complaint (e.g., arm) while ignoring secondary injuries (e.g., back), provides "work as tolerated" notes, and refuses to place employee off work, claiming they are "not allowed by law" due to the employer account relationship
3. **Employee seeks independent care**: When pain persists, employee goes to an independent ER/hospital on their own, where more thorough evaluation occurs and off-work orders are issued
4. **Employer resists WC claim**: Despite the employee being off work for more than 3 days (which should trigger WC claim initiation), the employer does not file a WC claim and instead forces the employee to use vacation time and PTO
5. **Employer cannot accommodate restrictions**: Even when medical restrictions are issued, the employer claims inability to accommodate them while still pushing the employee to come in
6. **Employee seeks legal representation**: The combination of obstruction, forced PTO usage, and unaccommodated restrictions drives the employee to contact a PI/WC firm

## Evidence

- In a workplace injury case (WC, DOI 2/2025), an employee of a major national shipping/logistics company fell down metal stairs, injuring her back and both arms/elbows. The employer transported her to Concentra (employer-directed occupational clinic), which examined only her arm, took X-rays, ignored her back injury, and provided a "work as tolerated" note. When pain persisted, the client independently visited a hospital where she was placed off work. She returned to Concentra for PT, where she was told they were "not allowed by law to place off work due to account with [employer]" — instead providing work restrictions. The employer could not accommodate restrictions but pushed her to come in, forcing her to use vacation time and PTO instead of initiating a WC claim. The client was off work since the DOI with no WC benefits. The firm noted that the employer "should have initiated WC claim once off work more than 3 days." The client was initially placed on hold while deciding whether to proceed, then signed and the firm engaged defense counsel. Later, the client became frustrated when calls to the assigned attorney went unreturned for over a month, leaving multiple voicemails with no response, and a second attorney eventually forwarded the voicemail to re-engage.

## Agent Guidance

1. During WC intake, ask whether the employer transported the employee to a specific clinic (employer-directed medical is a red flag)
2. Document whether the employer has initiated a WC claim — if not, note the date the employee was first off work (3-day threshold is critical)
3. Ask whether the client has been forced to use PTO/vacation instead of receiving TTD benefits
4. Identify whether the employer-directed clinic performed a complete evaluation or only addressed partial complaints
5. Document any statements from employer-directed clinics about limitations on placing employees off work
6. Ensure the client seeks independent medical evaluation for all injuries, not just those the occupational clinic addressed
7. Flag the case for attorney review if the employer has not filed a WC claim within the first week post-injury

## Pitfalls

- Assuming the employer-directed clinic's assessment is complete (they may have examined only one body part)
- Not documenting the employer's failure to initiate a WC claim — this is a key legal fact
- Delayed engagement: placing the case on hold while the client decides can result in lost evidence or statute issues
- Communication gaps during the hold period can cause client attrition — the client may feel abandoned before the case even starts
- Unreturned calls to the assigned attorney after engagement can compound the client's frustration with the employer's obstruction, creating a "nobody is helping me" perception

## Related

- [[workers-compensation-claim-patterns]] — standard WC claim workflow; this article covers when that workflow is obstructed
- [[workers-compensation-case-patterns]] — WC litigation and DWC proceedings
- [[client-frustration-escalation]] — WC obstruction cases generate rapid client frustration
- [[intake-and-onboarding-workflow]] — WC intake requires additional questions about employer claim status
"}

# Language Barriers Delay Case Development

> When a PI client does not speak English, the firm faces communication bottlenecks at every phase: intake information gathering, treatment verification, settlement discussions, and document execution. This barrier interacts with the companion case pattern and the co-counsel negotiation pattern, creating cascading delays.

## Pattern

Non-English-speaking clients create a distinct delay pattern:
1. **Intake**: Welcome call cannot be completed; case manager notes "client doesn't speak English" and defers to a companion or family member
2. **File Setup**: Companion case must be uploaded first so a bilingual contact can be designated as point of contact
3. **Treatment Verification**: Confirming treatment providers and obtaining information requires going through a proxy
4. **Negotiation**: When co-counsel from other firms are involved (multi-plaintiff or referral cases), language-matched attorneys may handle direct client communication while the primary firm manages the claim

## Evidence

- In an MVA case (negotiation phase), the client did not speak English. During the initial welcome call, the case manager noted the client "doesn't speak English, will wait for the companion's case to be uploaded and will make it as the point of contact." The police report was not yet available on BuyCrash either, compounding the delay. The case eventually involved co-counsel negotiation with two other attorneys (from separate firms), suggesting the language barrier may have contributed to the multi-firm structure. The case took approximately 18 months from DOI to settlement acceptance, with the attorney ultimately communicating settlement details to co-counsel via email: "[total] in meds. Closed head injury. No UIM."

## Linked Concepts

- [[intake-and-onboarding-workflow]] — language barrier blocks the standard welcome call
- [[companion-case-management]] — companion case may be needed to provide a bilingual point of contact
- [[client-communication-through-family]] — family members or companions serve as interpreters
- [[multi-carrier-negotiation]] — co-counsel structures may partially address the language gap
"}

    18|# Workers' Compensation Claim Patterns
    19|
    20|> Workers' compensation (WC) cases follow a fundamentally different process than auto accident PI cases. WC claims involve an employer's insurance carrier that must approve treatment, authorize specific providers, and pay Temporary Total Disability (TTD) benefits. Unlike BI claims where the firm collects records and sends a demand, WC cases require ongoing adjuster interaction to authorize each step of treatment.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|### WC Intake Differences
    25|1. **Employer notification**: Unlike auto accidents, the client must have notified their employer of the workplace injury
    26|2. **WC carrier identification**: The employer's WC carrier (not the client's personal auto/health insurer) is the primary claim target
    27|3. **LOR sent to WC carrier**: Letter of Representation is sent to the WC adjuster via email and fax
    28|4. **Claim acceptance**: The WC adjuster must formally accept the claim before treatment is authorized
    29|
    30|### WC Treatment Authorization
    31|Unlike PI cases where the client simply goes to a provider:
    32|1. The WC adjuster must approve the treating provider
    33|2. The firm may need to call the provider to confirm the approval was received
    34|3. If approval is not received by the provider, the firm must fax the approval directly
    35|4. The client's preferred provider may require formal WC approval before scheduling
    36|
    37|### TTD (Temporary Total Disability) Benefits
    38|1. TTD payments are periodic wage-replacement benefits during recovery
    39|2. Payments are based on the Average Weekly Wage (AWW)
    40|3. Payment amounts and timing must be tracked — missed or short payments require immediate follow-up with the adjuster
    41|4. AWW disputes may require filing a formal WC claim to get an accurate wage calculation
    42|
    43|### AWW (Average Weekly Wage) Disputes
    44|When the employer or carrier calculates the AWW incorrectly:
    45|1. The firm identifies the discrepancy (e.g., from employer records or client-reported wages)
    46|2. A formal WC claim may need to be filed with the state to correct the AWW
    47|3. This affects the TTD rate retroactively — past payments may need adjustment
    48|4. Resolution requires employer wage records and possibly payroll documentation
    49|
    50|### WC Deposition and Litigation

    51|WC cases may involve:
    52|- Defense counsel retained by the WC carrier
    53|- Deposition scheduling directly with defense counsel
    54|- Rescheduling is common when defense counsel needs to coordinate

    31|### Parallel Management
    32|- The UIM adjuster is separate from the PIP adjuster at the same carrier
    33|- The UIM claim number may be the same as or different from the PIP claim number
    34|- The UIM complaint is filed in the appropriate court (may be different jurisdiction than BI)
    35|- UIM litigation follows standard discovery and deposition procedures
    36|- The UIM carrier files an answer to the complaint and may retain defense counsel
    37|
    38|### Litigation
    39|- UIM complaints are filed against the client's own insurer — an adversarial posture against the client's own carrier
    40|- A courtesy copy of the complaint should be sent to the UIM adjuster
    41|- Discovery includes the plaintiff's first set of interrogatories/production requests to the UIM carrier
    42|- UIM litigation can proceed independently from BI settlement
    43|
    44|## Evidence
    45|
    46|- In Case G (auto accident, DOI 7/30/2023), the client's extensive injuries (including $45,830+ hospital bills, physical therapy, chiropractic care, and surgery) exceeded the at-fault driver's BI policy limits (State Farm, claim #1754K417G). The client had UIM coverage through Allstate Insurance (claim #723447272). The UIM claim required separate management:
    47|  - PIP adjuster (Margaret Sharp) handled PIP claim through Allstate
    48|  - UIM adjuster (Nick Burchette) handled the UIM claim separately
    49|  - A UIM complaint was filed in Fayette Circuit Court (24-CI-03580, "McCay v. Allstate Property and Casualty Insurance")
    50|  - Allstate filed an answer to the complaint
    51|  - A consent-to-settle form was obtained from Allstate before the BI claim could proceed
    52|  - Discovery commenced with plaintiff's first set of discovery to Allstate
    53|  - The case involved $319.82 in court filing fees, plus expenses for EvenUP ($500) and Digital Owl ($495) for case evaluation
    54|  - The client also had a Humana health insurance lien ($11,809) and pre-settlement funding ($31,200 from Legal Funding Partners)
    55|  - Defense counsel (Jennifer Ann Peterson) was retained by Allstate
    56|  - A separate "waiving subrogation" document was filed
    57|- In an auto accident case (DOI 10/2023, KY), the client's injuries included treatment at 8 providers (ER, chiropractic at multiple locations, radiology). The at-fault driver's BI coverage (through a state mutual insurer) was insufficient, triggering a UM claim through the client's own national carrier. A separate UM claim number was assigned and a distinct adjuster handled it. PIP was also through the same national carrier with yet another adjuster (the PIP team). The case entered litigation with all three insurance tracks active. This confirms the three-adjuster pattern at a single carrier: BI adjuster at the at-fault party's carrier, PIP team adjuster at the client's carrier, and UM adjuster at the client's carrier — each requiring separate tracking.
    58|- In an auto accident case (DOI 9/2021, KY), the case involved a UIM claim alongside the BI claim. The UIM carrier (a national insurance company) was the same as the PIP carrier. The UIM claim had its own claim number (same as PIP) but required a dedicated adjuster. Multiple adjuster reassignments occurred on the UIM claim. The case entered litigation after negotiation produced inadequate offers. This confirms that UIM adjuster reassignment follows the same pattern as BI adjuster reassignment, with the "fresh eyes" effect potentially unlocking movement.
    59|- In an MVA case (DOI 1/2023, KY), the at-fault driver carried Root Auto Insurance (BI), while the client's own Allstate policy provided both PIP and UM coverage. The UM claim shared the same claim number as PIP but had a different adjuster. The UM claim was designated "demand_pending" while the BI was "no_demand" — the primary recovery path was against the client's own UM carrier because the at-fault driver's BI coverage was insufficient. The case entered litigation (Jefferson 24-CI-004424) as a UM lawsuit against Allstate, with defense counsel retained by Allstate. The client also had a child support enforcement lien ($46K+), creating an unusual lien composition alongside the UM claim. Correspondence between plaintiff's counsel and defense counsel regarding scheduling/discovery confirms that UM litigation follows standard civil litigation procedures.
    60|- In a multi-carrier MVA case (DOI 1/2023, KY), the client had UIM coverage through Progressive (same carrier as PIP and BI). The BI claim against KFB was settled, while the UIM claim against Progressive went to litigation. The UIM adjuster was distinct from both the PIP adjuster and the BI adjuster — confirming the three-adjuster-at-one-carrier pattern. The case also had Medicare and health insurance liens, showing how UIM cases layer complexity on top of already complex lien management.
    61|- In Case Q (auto accident, VA veteran, DOI 5/2023), a three-carrier structure was observed: BI (Kentucky Farm Bureau), PIP (Everett Cash Mutual Insurance via a third-party administrator), and UIM (Erie Insurance Group). The UIM claim was managed by a dedicated adjuster at Erie. While the BI claim was in active litigation (Morgan County Circuit Court 24-CI-00065), the UIM claim remained on a parallel track with separate correspondence to the UIM adjuster. The case demonstrates that UIM can coexist with VA liens — the firm had to manage BI litigation, UIM parallel negotiation, PIP benefit tracking, AND a $86K+ VA lien simultaneously. The attorney was working on "distribution of funds" as of early 2025, indicating the BI component may have settled while UIM remained open. This confirms the pattern where UIM claims outlast BI resolution.
    62|- In an MVA case (DOI 7/2025), the BI carrier tendered its policy limits ($25K). After settling the BI claim, a COOTS (consent-to-settle) letter was sent, and a UM claim was opened with the client's own carrier (same carrier as PIP). The UM adjuster was assigned separately from the PIP adjuster. The firm began corresponding with the UM adjuster about medical records to support the UM claim. This confirms the standard BI-exhaustion-to-UIM-transition workflow: BI settles at limits → COOTS letter → UM claim opened → separate adjuster engagement → records provided to UM adjuster.
    63|- In an MVA case (DOI 10/2023), the companion case involved a BI claim and a UIM claim against the same national carrier. The firm contacted the carrier to confirm no arbitration had been initiated. The case was complicated by disputed liability — both drivers blamed each other. The UIM claim remained in limbo while the liability dispute was unresolved, confirming that disputed liability can stall UIM claims just as it stalls BI claims.
    64|- In an MVA case (DOI 6/2025), the case involved two BI carriers for two at-fault drivers, plus the client's own PIP carrier. While not a traditional UIM case, the multi-carrier structure demonstrates the same parallel-track management patterns. The firm tracked separate claim numbers and adjusters for each carrier independently.
    65|
    66|## Agent Guidance
    67|
    68|1. At intake, identify the client's own auto insurance UIM coverage limits
    69|2. Compare the at-fault driver's BI limits to estimated damages — if damages likely exceed BI limits, flag for UIM

This confirms the standard BI-exhaustion-to-UIM-transition workflow: BI settles at limits → COOTS letter → UM claim opened → separate adjuster engagement → records provided to UM adjuster.
 - In an MVA case (DOI 10/2023), the companion case involved a BI claim and a UIM claim against the same national carrier. The firm contacted the carrier to confirm no arbitration had been initiated. The case was complicated by disputed liability — both drivers blamed each other. The UIM claim remained in limbo while the liability dispute was unresolved, confirming that disputed liability can stall UIM claims just as it stalls BI claims.
 - In an MVA case (DOI 6/2025), the case involved two BI carriers for two at-fault drivers, plus the client's own PIP carrier. While not a traditional UIM case, the multi-carrier structure demonstrates the same parallel-track management patterns. The firm tracked separate claim numbers and adjusters for each carrier independently.
+- In an MVA case (closed/settlement, DOI 12/2024), the client had three insurance tracks: BI (at-fault driver's carrier), PIP (client's carrier), and UIM (same carrier as PIP, same claim number). The BI claim was settled, and the firm sent records/billing to the UIM adjuster requesting a "good faith offer upon completion" of review. The UIM adjuster responded and acknowledged receipt. A "Demand Sent - Follow up with BI Adjuster" note indicated the demand process was initiated. The BI claim subsequently settled, triggering a COOTS letter. The UIM process continued in parallel with its own adjuster, confirming the standard BI-settle-then-UIM-pursue workflow. The case also showed a companion spouse receiving the same treatment — both spouses' UIM claims were managed through the same carrier with the same adjuster but evaluated independently.

{"content": "    40|- VA liens, Medicare conditional payments, health insurance subrogation
    41|- Must be identified, quantified, and factored into settlement math
    42|- Payment may come from PIP (before settlement) or from settlement proceeds
    43|- Federal liens (VA, Medicare) have different legal frameworks than private liens
    44|
    45|### Coordination Points
    46|
    47|The critical coordination moments are:
    48|
    49|1. **File Setup**: Identify ALL insurance streams and liens. Set up separate tracking for each.
    50|2. **Bill Routing**: Each medical bill must be routed to the correct payer:
    51|   - VA treatment bills → VA lien (may be paid by PIP)
    52|   - Private provider bills → PIP first, then any remainder to health insurance
    53|   - Bills not covered by PIP/health insurance → BI demand specials
    54|3. **PIP Directing to Pay VA Lien**: PIP carriers can be directed to pay VA liens directly, effectively using the client's PIP benefits to satisfy the government lien. This reduces the VA lien's impact on the BI settlement.
    55|4. **Demand Preparation**: Before sending the BI demand, you need:
    56|   - Updated PIP ledger showing all payments
    57|   - Current VA lien amount (adjusted for any PIP payments)
    58|   - All medical specials from all providers
    59|5. **Settlement Math**: The BI settlement must account for remaining unpaid liens, attorney fees, and case costs. Multi-carrier cases have more deductions, often leaving less for the client.
    60|
    61|### The "New Money" vs. "Total Value" Distinction
    62|
    63|In multi-carrier cases, the BI adjuster may frame offers as "new money" — meaning money on top of what PIP and other sources have already paid. This distinction is important:
    64|- Total medical specials: Sum of all treatment costs
    65|- Already paid by PIP: Reduces what the BI settlement needs to cover
    66|- VA lien amount: Must be satisfied (may already be paid by PIP)
    67|- "New money" from BI: What the BI carrier pays in addition to PIP payments
    68|- Client's net: New money minus attorney fees, costs, and any remaining liens
    69|
    70|", "total_lines": 118, "file_size": 8214, "truncated": true, "hint": "Use offset=70 to continue reading (showing 40-69 of 118 lines)", "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

messages.content:
{"content": "    70|## Evidence
    71|
    72|- In Case D, the case involved three carriers and a government lien simultaneously:
    73|  - BI claim with a small regional carrier (at-fault party's insurer)
    74|  - PIP claim with a national carrier (client's own insurer)
    75|  - VA lien managed by VA OGC Revenue Law Group ($4,437.13)
    76|  - The PIP carrier was directed to pay the VA lien directly, which it did
    77|  - The BI adjuster framed his offer as "new money" of $4,200 on top of the VA payment, saying "the VA wants their money back" and noting the PIP had already handled the VA balance
    78|  - Later adjusters increased the offer, but also introduced defenses (attributing treatment to an unrelated incident)
    79|  - The final settlement math required tracking: PIP payments, VA lien satisfaction, chiro bills (disputed for relatedness), PT bills, and the BI offer
    80|- The PIP adjuster disputed the relatedness of chiropractic treatment, noting records indicated the treatment was for a condition that "gotten worse a month prior for no apparent reason" and requesting "additional documentation indicating relatedness."
    81|- In an auto accident case (DOI 10/2023, KY), a three-track insurance structure was active: BI (state mutual carrier), UM (national carrier), and PIP (same national carrier as UM). Each track had a separate adjuster. The BI carrier's claim was independent from the UM claim, but the UM and PIP claims shared a carrier with different adjusters and potentially the same claim number. The case entered litigation with all three tracks active simultaneously. Health insurance lien identification (Premera BCBS) and a carrier's own subrogation lien ($32.83) added to the complexity. This confirms that UM cases compound multi-carrier coordination: BI + UM + PIP = 3 adjusters minimum, plus liens.
    82|- In an auto accident case (DOI 10/2024, KY), a multi-carrier structure involved two BI carriers (state mutual + regional insurer) and a PIP carrier (regional insurer, same as one BI carrier). One BI carrier settled quickly while the other remained in negotiation. The PIP carrier was also the second BI carrier. Three adjusters were tracked across these claims. The settled BI amount had to be factored into the ongoing negotiation with the second carrier. This demonstrates the split-settlement math challenge: when one carrier settles, the remaining carrier's negotiation must account for what has already been recovered.
    83|
    84|## Agent Guidance
    85|
    86|1. At file setup, create a coordination matrix listing every carrier, claim number, adjuster, and lien:
    87|   | Stream | Carrier | Claim # | Adjuster | Status |
    88|   |--------|---------|---------|----------|--------|
    89|   | BI | [Carrier] | [#] | [Name] | [Phase] |
    90|   | PIP | [Carrier] | [#] | [Name] | [Phase] |
    91|   | VA Lien | VA OGC | [GCL#] | [Name] | [Phase] |
    92|2. Track bill routing: for each medical bill, record which carrier(s) it was submitted to and the payment status.
    93|3. When a VA lien is identified and PIP is available, discuss with the attorney whether to direct PIP to pay the VA lien.
    94|4. Before preparing the BI demand, ensure you have current ledgers/amounts from ALL streams.
    95|5. When presenting settlement offers to the attorney, show the complete multi-carrier picture:
    96|   - BI offer (new money)
    97|   - PIP payments already made
    98|   - VA/lien status (satisfied by PIP or outstanding)
    99|   - Net to client after all deductions
   100|6. When a PIP adjuster disputes relatedness of treatment, obtain additional documentation from the provider and resubmit.
   101|7. Monitor SOL separately — the BI statute may expire while you are still coordinating VA records/liens.
   102|
   103|## Pitfalls
   104|
   105|- Not identifying all insurance streams at intake
   106|- Losing track of which bills have been submitted to which carrier
   107|- Not understanding the "new money" distinction when evaluating BI offers
   108|- Letting VA lien delays hold up the entire BI negotiation
   109|- Not using PIP to pay VA liens when PIP benefits are available
   110|- Assuming the PIP adjuster will accept all bills as accident-related (they audit for relatedness too)
   111|- Not having complete ledgers from all carriers before preparing the BI demand
   112|
   113|## Related
   114|
   115|- [[pip-claims-management]] — PIP claim management as one stream in multi-carrier coordination
   116|- [[va-lien-management]] — VA lien as a unique stream requiring distinct handling
   117|- [[lien-management-health-insurance]] — health insurance lien as another stream
   118|- [[pre-settlement-funding-obligations]] — additional deduction from settlement that compounds multi-carrier complexity
   119|", "total_lines": 118, "file_size": 8214, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

Each carrier had its own adjuster. Liability was accepted by one carrier while the others remained unresolved. The firm tracked PIP ledger requests separately from BI correspondence, confirming the independence of PIP and BI management tracks even at different carriers.

## 2026-04-12 — Batch Compilation (fidel-antonio-bueso-sandoval, dewayne-ward, elizabeth-lindsey, jaysha-aponte, shannon-lankswert)

**Compiler**: Wiki Compiler Agent (subagent)
**Cases Processed**: 5 (cumulative: 35+)
**Source Data**:
- Case Y (auto accident, negotiation, DOI 6/2024): 198 activity logs — KY MVA, non-English-speaking client (Spanish), companion case pending, co-counsel negotiation with 2 other attorneys from separate firms, same BI and PIP carrier (State Farm), no UIM, closed head injury, SOL countdown (180-day and 90-day alerts), settlement accepted via email with carrier, police report delayed (BuyCrash unavailable)
- Case Z (auto accident, litigation, DOI 8/2023): 186 activity logs — KY MVA, companion case (3 passengers: client plus 2 companions), 3 BI carriers (State Farm, Elco, Liberty Mutual PIP), Floyd County litigation, depositions scheduled and mediation planned, Conduent health insurance lien, multi-plaintiff lawsuit styled as "et al. v. [Defendant]", deposition scheduling with defense counsel, mediation correspondence
- Case AA (auto accident, closed/settlement, DOI 12/2024): 183 activity logs — KY MVA, 3 insurance tracks (BI Progressive, PIP Auto Owners, UIM Auto Owners), companion case (spouse), BI settled then COOTS letter sent, UIM demand sent separately to own carrier's adjuster, Rawlings health insurance lien, records/billing sent to UIM adjuster with "good faith offer" request, fax-based records collection
- Case AB (auto accident, treatment, DOI 3/2025): 180 activity logs — KY MVA, 3 BI carriers (Mobilitas, GEICO, Progressive PIP), liability accepted by Mobilitas, multiple medical providers (6 including chiropractic, EMS, ER, family health center), records follow-up required for chiropractic provider (Starlite), PIP ledger request to Progressive, treatment completed but records still outstanding
- Case AC (workers compensation, treatment, DOI 2/2025): 175 activity logs — KY workplace injury (fall at major logistics employer), Liberty Mutual WC carrier, employer-directed medical at Concentra, employer obstruction of WC claim (forcing PTO/vacation instead of TTD), multiple medical providers (4 including occupational clinic, hospital, injury rehab, rehab center), defense counsel assigned, client frustration pattern (unreturned calls over 1+ month), Anthem BCBS health insurance lien

**Articles Created**: 1 concept article, 1 connection article
**Articles Updated**: 23 existing concept articles, 4 connection articles

### Concept Created
1. `employer-wc-claim-obstruction` — New pattern: employer resistance to initiating WC claims, forced PTO/vacation usage, employer-directed occupational clinic providing incomplete evaluations, restriction accommodation refusal. Distinct from standard WC workflow.

### Connection Created
1. `language-barrier-delays-case-development` — How non-English-speaking clients create cascading delays at intake, file setup, and negotiation; interaction with companion case and co-counsel patterns.

### Concepts Updated (with evidence_count changes)
1. `intake-and-onboarding-workflow` — 20->25 (added Spanish-speaking client intake, WC employer obstruction intake, multi-BI-carrier intake, companion spouse intake, records-from-buycrash delay)

7. `companion-case-management` — 4->8 (added non-English companion as point of contact, inter-firm companion coordination, spouse companion with shared PIP/UIM, multi-carrier companion with independent liability determinations)
8. `client-communication-through-family` — 10->12 (added non-English-speaking client relying on companion for all communication, WC client voicemail escalation forwarded between attorneys)

12. `client-frustration-escalation` — 14->16 (added WC client 1+ month unreturned calls pattern with voicemail transcription, companion spouse follow-up calls for simultaneous offers)
13. `lien-management-health-insurance` — 13->16 (added Rawlings Company health insurance lien in UIM case, Conduent lien in multi-defendant litigation, Anthem BCBS lien in WC case)
14. `demand-preparation-workflow` — 8->10 (added UIM records-as-demand pattern with "good faith offer" request, co-counsel demand value summary via email)
15. `multi-carrier-multi-defendant-cases` — 7->10 (added 3-BI-carrier case with Mobilitas/GEICO/Progressive, State Farm/Elco/Liberty Mutual multi-defendant, co-counsel 3-firm negotiation)

22. `workers-compensation-case-patterns` — 3->4 (added employer-directed medical obstruction case with defense counsel)
23. `medical-bill-collections-anxiety` — 6->7 (added WC client financial pressure from forced PTO usage instead of TTD)

### Connections Updated
1. `client-frustration-drives-premature-settlement` — 10->12 (added WC unreturned calls, co-counsel delay)
2. `records-completion-gates-demand` — 10->13 (added chiropractic records delay blocking demand, UIM records gating offer request, multi-provider records collection)
3. `litigation-as-negotiation-leverage` — 9->11 (added multi-plaintiff litigation coordination, deposition/mediation scheduling as leverage)
4. `split-settlement-complicates-multi-carrier-math` — 2->3 (added BI-settled-then-UIM-continues pattern with COOTS letter)

### Confidence Upgrades
- `companion-case-management`: medium (8 cases — approaching high threshold)
- `multi-carrier-multi-defendant-cases`: medium->high (10 cases)

### Key New Patterns Identified
- **Employer WC claim obstruction is a genuine new pattern**: Unlike standard WC cases where the carrier promptly accepts the claim, some employers actively resist initiating WC claims, directing employees to employer-controlled clinics and forcing PTO usage instead of TTD benefits. This requires a fundamentally different intake approach.
- **Co-counsel multi-firm negotiation is a distinct variant**: Three attorneys from three firms negotiating jointly with a single BI carrier — the coordination challenge shifts from managing carriers to managing co-counsel. Settlement value must be communicated concisely between firms via email.
- **Non-English-speaking clients create a cascade of delays**: From blocked welcome calls to dependent companion cases to co-counsel communication structures, the language barrier touches every phase.
- **Three-BI-carrier cases with independent liability are newly observed**: Unlike prior multi-carrier cases where all claims were against a single at-fault driver's insurer, this batch shows cases with 3 separate BI carriers for 2-3 at-fault parties, each with independent liability determinations.
- **UIM "good faith offer" request is an informal demand variant**: Rather than a formal demand letter, the firm sends records/billing to the UIM adjuster with a request for a "good faith offer upon completion" of review — a softer approach than BI demands.
- **COOTS letter as BI-to-UIM transition marker**: The Consent to Settle (COOTS) letter sent after BI settlement formally triggers the UIM claim track. This is a concrete procedural step confirmed across multiple cases.
- **WC client frustration escalates rapidly with unreturned calls**: In the WC case, the client went from initial engagement to leaving frustrated voicemails within weeks when the assigned attorney didn't return calls. The client's voicemail explicitly stated she had "been trying to call back... left several voicemails... not gotten a response from nobody."

- **UIM claim management**: Another case confirms the BI-settle-then-UIM workflow with COOTS letter (now medium confidence, 7 cases)
- **Companion case coordination**: Four new companion case patterns from different angles (language, inter-firm, UIM, multi-carrier)
- **Multi-carrier negotiation**: Co-counsel variant adds a genuinely new dimension to the pattern
- **Workers' compensation patterns**: First employer obstruction case provides a contrasting pattern to standard WC intake

### Remaining Gaps
- Co-counsel fee splitting: No data on how fees are allocated among co-counsel firms
- Non-English-speaking client long-term communication: Only one case observed; need more to understand ongoing patterns
- Employer WC obstruction resolution: Only one case, still in treatment phase; no WC claim outcome data
- Three-BI-carrier settlement coordination: No case has completed settlement across all three BI carriers
- UIM "good faith offer" outcomes: No completed UIM negotiations using this informal demand approach
- Concentra/occupational clinic independent verification: How firms challenge occupational clinic assessments"}

+- **Employer WC claim obstruction is a genuine new pattern**: Unlike standard WC cases where the carrier promptly accepts the claim, some employers actively resist initiating WC claims, directing employees to employer-controlled clinics and forcing PTO usage instead of TTD benefits. This requires a fundamentally different intake approach.
+- **Co-counsel multi-firm negotiation is a distinct variant**: Three attorneys from three firms negotiating jointly with a single BI carrier — the coordination challenge shifts from managing carriers to managing co-counsel. Settlement value must be communicated concisely between firms via email.
